Made with MyST

The Rigid Rotor

What You Need to Know

  • The rigid rotor model serves as a prototype for understanding the quantization of rotational degrees of freedom in molecules. We use a spherical coordinate system to exploit the spherical symmetry of the problem, effectively reducing the system’s dimensionality.

  • Solving the Schrödinger equation in spherical coordinates yields eigenfunctions in the form of spherical harmonics. The resulting energy eigenvalues exhibit degeneracy with respect to one of the quantum numbers.

  • To compute rotational spectra, the moment of inertia of the molecule must be known. For diatomic molecules, this is simply I=μr2I = \mu r^2 I=μr2, where μ\mu μ is the reduced mass and rr r is the bond distance. For polyatomic molecules, the calculation is more complex, as it must account for the spatial distribution of mass.

  • Microwave spectroscopy is directly connected to this model, with spectral lines predicted to occur at equal intervals of 2B~2\tilde{B} 2B~.

  • The selection rule is established through the recursion relation of spherical harmonics and requires ΔJ=±1\Delta J = \pm 1 ΔJ=±1 and ΔMJ=0,±1\Delta M_J = 0, \pm 1 ΔMJ​=0,±1.

  • Coupling with vibrational modes leads to rovibronic transitions, necessitating the inclusion of vibrational quantum numbers for a comprehensive description of transition frequencies.

  • Rotational motion can cause slight changes in bond length, known as the centrifugal distortion effect. This effect can be accounted for by adding a centrifugal correction term to the rigid rotor model.

Quantum rigid rotor and angular momentum operator¶

  • The Hamiltonian for the rigid rotor model is the kinetic energy operator of an effective mass μ\muμ which rotates around a sphere of radius r=constr=constr=const.

  • To incorporate the constraint r=constr=constr=const it is more convenient to adopt spherical coordinates (x,y,z)→(r,θ,ϕ)(x,y,z)\rightarrow (r,\theta,\phi)(x,y,z)→(r,θ,ϕ). The full Laplacian in spherical coordinates is:

  • In spherical coordinates the Hamiltonian is more conveniently expressed in terms of the angular momentum operator as opposed to the linear momentum operator:

H^=−ℏ22μ∇x,y,z2=−ℏ22μr2∇θ,ϕ2=L^22I\hat{H}=-\frac{\hbar^2}{2\mu}\nabla_{x,y,z}^2 = -\frac{\hbar^2}{2\mu r^2}\nabla_{\theta,\phi}^2=\frac{\hat{L}^2}{2I}H^=−2μℏ2​∇x,y,z2​=−2μr2ℏ2​∇θ,ϕ2​=2IL^2​
(1)

  • Where I=μr2I=\mu r^2I=μr2 is the moment of inertia and we have identified the angular momentum operator as:

L^=−iℏ∇θ,ϕ\hat{L}= -i\hbar \nabla_{\theta,\phi}L^=−iℏ∇θ,ϕ​
(2)

Quantum numbers (J,MJ)(J,M_J)(J,MJ​) for quantizing (θ,ϕ)(\theta,\phi)(θ,ϕ) coordinate pair.¶

  • Having written down the Hamiltonian we now solve it, anticipating two quantum numbers for two coordinates.

  • The eigenfunctions turn out to be well-known special functions called spherical harmonics Y(θ,ϕ)Y(\theta,\phi)Y(θ,ϕ):

H^Y(θ,ϕ)=EJ,mY(θ,ϕ)\hat{H}Y(\theta, \phi)=E_{J,m}Y(\theta,\phi)H^Y(θ,ϕ)=EJ,m​Y(θ,ϕ)
(3)

  • We are once again able to separate two angular variables and solve the resulting ODE exactly.

  • We expect the energy to depend on two quantum numbers JJJ and MJM_JMJ​, which quantize rotational motion across the θ\thetaθ and ϕ\phiϕ angles.

Rotational states of molecules are quantized¶

Eigenvalues of Rotational States
EJ=ℏ22IJ(J+1)=BJ(J+1)E_J = \frac{\hbar^2}{2I}J(J+1) = BJ(J+1)EJ​=2Iℏ2​J(J+1)=BJ(J+1)
(4)

  • The rotational constant (has units of energy) is defined

B=h28π2I,B = \frac{h^2}{8\pi^2 I},B=8π2Ih2​,
(5)

  • Solving the rigid rotor problem reveals that rotational energy eigenvalues depend only on the quantum number JJJ.

  • Each energy level is therefore (2J+1)(2J + 1)(2J+1)-fold degenerate, corresponding to the allowed values of the magnetic quantum number MJM_JMJ​.

  • The quantization of rotational motion arises from the cyclic boundary condition rather than from any potential energy term (which is zero for a free rotor).

    Φ(0)=Φ(2π),\Phi(0) = \Phi(2\pi),Φ(0)=Φ(2π),
    (6)

  • There is no rotational zero-point energy, since the J=0J = 0J=0 state is allowed.

  • The ground-state rotational wavefunction corresponds to an isotropic distribution, with equal probability for all orientations.

Example

What are the reduced mass and moment of inertia of H35ClH^{35}ClH35Cl? The equilibrium internuclear distance ReR_eRe​ is 127.5 pm. What are the values of L,LzL, L_zL,Lz​ and EEE for the state with J=1J = 1J=1? The atomic masses are: mH=1.673470⋅10−27m_H = 1.673470 \cdot 10^{-27}mH​=1.673470⋅10−27 kg and mCl=5.806496⋅10−26m_{Cl} = 5.806496 \cdot 10^{-26}mCl​=5.806496⋅10−26 kg.

Solution

First we calculate the reduced mass:

μ=mHm35ClmH+m35Cl=(1.673470×10−27 kg)(5.806496×10−26 kg)(1.673470×10−27 kg)+(5.806496×10−26 kg)\mu = \frac{m_{\textnormal{H}}m_{^{35}\textnormal{Cl}}}{m_{\textnormal{H}} + m_{^{35}\textnormal{Cl}}} = \frac{(1.673470\times 10^{-27}\textnormal{ kg})(5.806496\times 10^{-26}\textnormal{ kg})}{(1.673470\times 10^{-27}\textnormal{ kg}) + (5.806496\times 10^{-26}\textnormal{ kg})}μ=mH​+m35Cl​mH​m35Cl​​=(1.673470×10−27 kg)+(5.806496×10−26 kg)(1.673470×10−27 kg)(5.806496×10−26 kg)​
(7)
=1.62665×10−27 kg= 1.62665\times 10^{-27}\textnormal{ kg}=1.62665×10−27 kg
(8)
I=μRe2=(1.626×10−27 kg)(127.5×10−12 m)2=2.644×10−47 kg m2I = \mu R_e^2 = (1.626\times 10^{-27}\textnormal{ kg})(127.5\times 10^{-12}\textnormal{ m})^2 = 2.644\times 10^{-47}\textnormal{ kg m}^2I=μRe2​=(1.626×10−27 kg)(127.5×10−12 m)2=2.644×10−47 kg m2
(9)

LLL and LzL_zLz​ are given by the eigenvalue expressions of the respective operators:

L=J(J+1)ℏ=2(1.054×10−34 Js)=1.491×10−34 JsL = \sqrt{J(J+1)}\hbar = \sqrt{2}\left(1.054\times 10^{-34}\textnormal{ Js}\right) = 1.491\times 10^{-34}\textnormal{ Js}L=J(J+1)​ℏ=2​(1.054×10−34 Js)=1.491×10−34 Js
(10)
Lz=−ℏ,0,ℏ (three possible values)L_z = -\hbar,0,\hbar\textnormal{ (three possible values)}Lz​=−ℏ,0,ℏ (three possible values)
(11)

The energy of the J=1J = 1J=1 level is given by the eigenvalue expression of the Hamiltonian:

E=ℏ22IJ(J+1)=ℏ2I=4.206×10−22 J=21 cm−1E = \frac{\hbar^2}{2I}J(J+1) = \frac{\hbar^2}{I} = 4.206\times 10^{-22}\textnormal{ J} = 21\textnormal{ cm}^{-1}E=2Iℏ2​J(J+1)=Iℏ2​=4.206×10−22 J=21 cm−1
(12)

This rotational spacing can be observed, for example, in the gas-phase infrared spectrum of HCl.

Rotational spectra of diatomic molecules¶

  • We assume that the molecule is a rigid rotor, which means that the molecular geometry does not change during rotation. We have solved this problem already.

  • Energies are typically expressed in wavenumber units (cm−1cm^{-1}cm−1, although the basic SI unit is m−1m^{-1}m−1) by dividing EEE by hchchc. The use of wavenumber units is denoted by a tilde above the variable (e.g., ν~\tilde{\nu}ν~).

  • Within the rigid rotor approximation we can derive selection rules by computing the transition dipole moment using properties of the spherical harmonics.

Rotational energies in spectroscopic units
E~r(J)=Erhc=B~J(J+1){\tilde{E}_r(J) = \frac{E_r}{hc} = \tilde{B} J(J+1)}E~r​(J)=hcEr​​=B~J(J+1)
(13)

  • the rotational constant expressed in cm−1cm^{-1}cm−1 units is given by:

B~=h8π2Ic{\tilde{B} = \frac{h}{8\pi^2Ic}}B~=8π2Ich​
(14)
Selection rules for rigid rotor model

  • The molecule must possess a permanent dipole moment!

⟨J′∣μ∣J′′⟩≠0\langle J' | \mu | J'' \rangle \neq 0⟨J′∣μ∣J′′⟩=0
(15)

  • On top of that, only transitions between adjacent levels are allowed. For example, ΔJ=0\Delta J =0ΔJ=0 and ∣ΔJ∣>1|\Delta J| > 1∣ΔJ∣>1 transitions are forbidden.

ΔJ=J′−J=±1\boxed{\Delta J = J' - J = \pm 1}ΔJ=J′−J=±1​
(16)

Spectral lines and rotational constant determination¶

  • When we compute the energy spacing we see that it depends linearly on the quantum level JJJ. This means we can take one more difference and easily eliminate it, getting a constant:

νJ~=E~r(J+1)−E~r(J)=((J+1)(J+2)−J(J+1))B~=2B~(J+1){\tilde{\nu_J} = \tilde{E}_r(J + 1) - \tilde{E}_r(J) = \left((J+1)(J+2) - J(J+1)\right)\tilde{B} = 2\tilde{B}(J+1)}νJ​~​=E~r​(J+1)−E~r​(J)=((J+1)(J+2)−J(J+1))B~=2B~(J+1)
(17)
Spacing of adjacent spectral lines
ν~J+1−νJ~=2B~\tilde{\nu}_{J+1} - \tilde{\nu_J} = 2\tilde{B}ν~J+1​−νJ​~​=2B~
(18)

  • The successive line positions in the rotational spectrum are given by 2B~,4B~,6B~,...2\tilde{B}, 4\tilde{B}, 6\tilde{B},...2B~,4B~,6B~,.... Note that molecules with different atomic isotopes have different moments of inertia and hence different positions for the rotational lines.

Population of rotational states

Another factor that affects the line intensities in a rotational spectrum is related to the thermal population of the rotational levels. Thermal populations of the rotational levels is given by the Boltzmann distribution (for a collection of molecules):

fJ=gJe−hcE~r(J)/(kBT)∑J′gJ′e−hcE~r(J′)/(kBT)=gJe−hcE~r(J)/(kBT)q{f_J = \frac{g_Je^{-hc\tilde{E}_r(J) / (k_B T)}}{\sum\limits_{J'}g_{J'}e^{-hc\tilde{E}_r(J') / (k_B T)}} = \frac{g_Je^{-hc\tilde{E}_r(J) / (k_B T)}}{q}}fJ​=J′∑​gJ′​e−hcE~r​(J′)/(kB​T)gJ​e−hcE~r​(J)/(kB​T)​=qgJ​e−hcE~r​(J)/(kB​T)​
(19)

  • Here qqq is called the partition function, and gJ=2J+1g_J = 2J + 1gJ​=2J+1 is the degeneracy of state JJJ. A useful reference for the thermal energy is kTkTkT: if the energy of a state is much higher than this, it will not be thermally populated.

  • One expects the intensities to first increase as a function of the initial state JJJ, reach a maximum, and then decrease as the thermal populations fall off. In an absorption experiment, one can see the thermal populations of the initial rotational levels.

  • Note: for systems where the rotational degrees of freedom may exchange identical nuclei, an additional complication arises from the symmetry requirement on the nuclear wavefunction. Recall that bosons must have symmetric wavefunctions and fermions antisymmetric ones. We will not discuss this in more detail here.

Example

Calculate the relative populations of the first five rotational levels of the ground vibrational state of H35H^{35}H35Cl at 300 K. The ground vibrational state rotational constant B0=10.44B_0 = 10.44B0​=10.44 cm−1^{-1}−1.

Solution

The level populations are given by the Boltzmann distribution:

NJN0=(2J+1)e−hcJ(J+1)B~0/(kBT)\frac{N_J}{N_0} = \left(2J + 1\right)e^{-hcJ(J+1)\tilde{B}_0/(k_BT)}N0​NJ​​=(2J+1)e−hcJ(J+1)B~0​/(kB​T)
(20)

where N0N_0N0​ is the number of molecules in the rotational ground state. First we calculate the factor appearing in the exponent:

hcB~0kBT=(6.626×10−34 Js)(2.998×108 m/s)(10.44 cm−1)(102 cm/m)(1.3806×10−23 J K−1)(300 K)\frac{hc\tilde{B}_0}{k_BT} = \frac{(6.626\times 10^{-34}\textnormal{ Js})(2.998\times 10^8\textnormal{ m/s})(10.44\textnormal{ cm}^{-1})(10^2\textnormal{ cm/m})}{(1.3806\times 10^{-23}\textnormal{ J K}^{-1})(300\textnormal{ K})}kB​ThcB~0​​=(1.3806×10−23 J K−1)(300 K)(6.626×10−34 Js)(2.998×108 m/s)(10.44 cm−1)(102 cm/m)​
(21)
=5.007×10−2= 5.007\times 10^{-2}=5.007×10−2
(22)

Then, for example, for J=1J = 1J=1 we get:

N1N0=3e−2(5.007×10−2)=2.71\frac{N_1}{N_0} = 3e^{-2(5.007\times 10^{-2})} = 2.71N0​N1​​=3e−2(5.007×10−2)=2.71
(23)

The same way one can get the relative populations as: 1.00, 2.71, 3.70, 3.84, 3.31, and 2.45 for J=0,1,2,3,4,5J = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5J=0,1,2,3,4,5. Note that these are relative populations since we did not calculate the partition function qqq.

Ro-vibrational spectra, R, P and Q branches¶

  • Often we are interested in transitions among rotational levels that accompany excitation from the ground vibrational state, v=0→v=1v=0\rightarrow v=1v=0→v=1. This can be described by combining the rigid rotor and harmonic oscillator models:

E~v,J=ω~(v+1/2)+B~J(J+1)\tilde{E}_{v, J} = \tilde{\omega}(v+1/2)+\tilde{B}J(J+1)E~v,J​=ω~(v+1/2)+B~J(J+1)
(24)

  • Since at room temperature molecules mostly occupy the vibrational ground state, we are interested in rotational transitions taking place between the ground (v=0v=0v=0) and the first excited (v=1v=1v=1) vibrational states.

  • Rigid rotor model predicts different frequencies for absorption and emission transitions between any two rotational states JJJ and J′J'J′ given by ν~ΔJ=E~1,J′−E~0,J\tilde{\nu}_{\Delta J} = {\tilde{E}_{1,J'} - \tilde{E}_{0,J}}ν~ΔJ​=E~1,J′​−E~0,J​ where the J=0,1,2...J = 0,1,2...J=0,1,2... refers to the initial rotational state and J′J'J′ is the final state.

  • The transitions with ΔJ=+1\Delta J=+1ΔJ=+1 are called R branch:

ν~ΔJ=+1=ω~+2B~(J+1)\tilde{\nu}_{\Delta J=+1}=\tilde{\omega} + 2\tilde{B}(J+1)ν~ΔJ=+1​=ω~+2B~(J+1)
(25)

  • The transitions with ΔJ=−1\Delta J=-1ΔJ=−1 are called P branch:

ν~ΔJ=−1=ω~−2B~J\tilde{\nu}_{\Delta J=-1}=\tilde {\omega} - 2\tilde{B}Jν~ΔJ=−1​=ω~−2B~J
(26)

  • The Q-branch ΔJ=0\Delta J =0ΔJ=0 is predicted to be absent because it is forbidden by the selection rule of the rigid rotor model.

ν~ΔJ=0=ω~\tilde{\nu}_{\Delta J=0}=\tilde {\omega}ν~ΔJ=0​=ω~
(27)

Rigid rotor and real microwave spectra¶

Rovibronic Coupling¶

  • As a diatomic molecule vibrates, its bond length oscillates. Because the moment of inertia depends on the bond length, it also changes during vibration, leading to a variation in the rotational constant BBB.

  • In earlier approximations, we assumed that the rotational constants of the R(0)R(0)R(0) and P(1)P(1)P(1) transitions were identical. In reality, they differ due to this rovibrational coupling.

  • The dependence of BBB on the vibrational quantum number vvv can be expressed as:

    Bv=Be−αe(v+12),B_v = B_e - \alpha_e (v + \tfrac{1}{2}),Bv​=Be​−αe​(v+21​),
    (28)

  • where BeB_eBe​ is the equilibrium rotational constant (for a rigid rotor), and αe\alpha_eαe​ is the rotation–vibration coupling constant. From observed band spectra, one can determine B0B_0B0​, B1B_1B1​, and αe\alpha_eαe​ using combination differences.

  • R branch (ΔJ = +1) with rovibronic coupling:

ν~R=ω~+2B~1+(3B~1−B~0)J+(B~1−B~0)J2\tilde{\nu}_{R} = \tilde{\omega} + 2\tilde{B}_1 + (3\tilde{B}_1 - \tilde{B}_0)J + (\tilde{B}_1 - \tilde{B}_0)J^2ν~R​=ω~+2B~1​+(3B~1​−B~0​)J+(B~1​−B~0​)J2
(29)

  • P branch (ΔJ = −1) with rovibronic coupling:

ν~P=ω~−(B~1+B~0)J+(B~1−B~0)J2\tilde{\nu}_{P} = \tilde{\omega} - (\tilde{B}_1 + \tilde{B}_0)J + (\tilde{B}_1 - \tilde{B}_0)J^2ν~P​=ω~−(B~1​+B~0​)J+(B~1​−B~0​)J2
(30)

  • When B0=B1B_0 = B_1B0​=B1​, these reduce to the rigid-rotor–harmonic-oscillator expressions, as expected.

Centrifugal Distortion¶

  • Real molecules are not perfectly rigid. As rotational speed increases, the centrifugal force stretches the bond, increasing the moment of inertia and reducing the energy spacing between rotational levels.

  • This effect represents another type of rotation–vibration coupling, but it arises from the centrifugal stretching of the bond rather than from vibrational averaging of BBB.

  • The correction is introduced by adding a centrifugal distortion term to the rigid-rotor energy expression:

    E~r(J)=B~J(J+1)−D~J2(J+1)2,\tilde{E}_r(J) = \tilde{B} J(J+1) - \tilde{D} J^2 (J+1)^2,E~r​(J)=B~J(J+1)−D~J2(J+1)2,
    (31)

    where D~\tilde{D}D~ is the centrifugal distortion constant in cm−1cm^{-1}cm−1, and both B~\tilde{B}B~ and D~\tilde{D}D~ are positive.

  • The corresponding rotational transition frequencies become:

    ν~=E~r(J+1)−E~r(J)=2B~(J+1)−4D~(J+1)3,J=0,1,2,…\tilde{\nu} = \tilde{E}_r(J+1) - \tilde{E}_r(J) = 2\tilde{B}(J+1) - 4\tilde{D}(J+1)^3, \qquad J = 0, 1, 2, \ldotsν~=E~r​(J+1)−E~r​(J)=2B~(J+1)−4D~(J+1)3,J=0,1,2,…
    (32)

Key Difference in two rovibronic couplings¶

EffectPhysical OriginDepends onTypical Signature
Rovibronic couplingVibrational averaging of BBB due to bond-length oscillationVibrational quantum number vvvCauses BvB_vBv​ to decrease linearly with vvv
Centrifugal distortionBond stretching at high rotational speedsRotational quantum number JJJCauses energy levels to crowd at large JJJ

Problems¶

Problem 1¶

Consider a diatomic molecule with the following constants:

  • Vibrational constant: ωe=2100 cm−1\omega_e = 2100 \, \text{cm}^{-1}ωe​=2100cm−1

  • Rotational constant: Be=1.4 cm−1B_e = 1.4 \, \text{cm}^{-1}Be​=1.4cm−1

The molecule undergoes a transition from the vibrational ground state (v=0v = 0v=0) to the first excited vibrational state (v=1v = 1v=1).

  1. Calculate the wavenumbers of the PPP-branch transitions for J=1J = 1J=1 and J=2J = 2J=2 in the v=0→v=1v = 0 \rightarrow v = 1v=0→v=1 transition.

  2. Calculate the wavenumbers of the RRR-branch transitions for J=0J = 0J=0 and J=1J = 1J=1 in the v=0→v=1v = 0 \rightarrow v = 1v=0→v=1 transition.

  3. Explain the nature of the PPP- and RRR-branches in the context of rotational-vibrational spectroscopy and how they appear in the spectrum.

Solution

Part 1: PPP-Branch Transitions

The PPP-branch corresponds to transitions where ΔJ=−1\Delta J = -1ΔJ=−1. For a v=0→v=1v = 0 \rightarrow v = 1v=0→v=1 vibrational transition, the wavenumber of the PPP-branch transition from a state with rotational quantum number JJJ is given by:

ν~P(J)=ωe−Be[J(J−1)]\tilde{\nu}_{P(J)} = \omega_e - B_e \left[ J (J - 1) \right]ν~P(J)​=ωe​−Be​[J(J−1)]
(33)

  1. For J=1J = 1J=1:

    ν~P(1)=ωe−Be⋅1⋅(1−1)=ωe−Be⋅0=2100−0=2100 cm−1\tilde{\nu}_{P(1)} = \omega_e - B_e \cdot 1 \cdot (1 - 1) = \omega_e - B_e \cdot 0 = 2100 - 0 = 2100 \, \text{cm}^{-1}ν~P(1)​=ωe​−Be​⋅1⋅(1−1)=ωe​−Be​⋅0=2100−0=2100cm−1
    (34)

  2. For J=2J = 2J=2:

    ν~P(2)=ωe−Be⋅2⋅(2−1)=2100−1.4⋅2=2100−2.8=2097.2 cm−1\tilde{\nu}_{P(2)} = \omega_e - B_e \cdot 2 \cdot (2 - 1) = 2100 - 1.4 \cdot 2 = 2100 - 2.8 = 2097.2 \, \text{cm}^{-1}ν~P(2)​=ωe​−Be​⋅2⋅(2−1)=2100−1.4⋅2=2100−2.8=2097.2cm−1
    (35)

So, the wavenumbers for the PPP-branch transitions are:

  • J=1→J=0J = 1 \rightarrow J = 0J=1→J=0: 2100 cm−12100 \, \text{cm}^{-1}2100cm−1

  • J=2→J=1J = 2 \rightarrow J = 1J=2→J=1: 2097.2 cm−12097.2 \, \text{cm}^{-1}2097.2cm−1

Part 2: RRR-Branch Transitions

The RRR-branch corresponds to transitions where ΔJ=+1\Delta J = +1ΔJ=+1. For a v=0→v=1v = 0 \rightarrow v = 1v=0→v=1 vibrational transition, the wavenumber of the RRR-branch transition from a state with rotational quantum number JJJ is given by:

ν~R(J)=ωe+Be[(J+1)(J+2)]\tilde{\nu}_{R(J)} = \omega_e + B_e \left[ (J + 1)(J + 2) \right]ν~R(J)​=ωe​+Be​[(J+1)(J+2)]
(36)

  1. For J=0J = 0J=0:

    ν~R(0)=ωe+Be⋅(0+1)(0+2)=2100+1.4⋅2=2100+2.8=2102.8 cm−1\tilde{\nu}_{R(0)} = \omega_e + B_e \cdot (0 + 1)(0 + 2) = 2100 + 1.4 \cdot 2 = 2100 + 2.8 = 2102.8 \, \text{cm}^{-1}ν~R(0)​=ωe​+Be​⋅(0+1)(0+2)=2100+1.4⋅2=2100+2.8=2102.8cm−1
    (37)

  2. For J=1J = 1J=1:

    ν~R(1)=ωe+Be⋅(1+1)(1+2)=2100+1.4⋅6=2100+8.4=2108.4 cm−1\tilde{\nu}_{R(1)} = \omega_e + B_e \cdot (1 + 1)(1 + 2) = 2100 + 1.4 \cdot 6 = 2100 + 8.4 = 2108.4 \, \text{cm}^{-1}ν~R(1)​=ωe​+Be​⋅(1+1)(1+2)=2100+1.4⋅6=2100+8.4=2108.4cm−1
    (38)

So, the wavenumbers for the RRR-branch transitions are:

  • J=0→J=1J = 0 \rightarrow J = 1J=0→J=1: 2102.8 cm−12102.8 \, \text{cm}^{-1}2102.8cm−1

  • J=1→J=2J = 1 \rightarrow J = 2J=1→J=2: 2108.4 cm−12108.4 \, \text{cm}^{-1}2108.4cm−1

Part 3: Nature of the PPP- and RRR-Branches

In rotational-vibrational spectroscopy:

  • The PPP-branch consists of transitions where the rotational quantum number decreases by 1 (ΔJ=−1\Delta J = -1ΔJ=−1). These transitions appear at wavenumbers lower than the vibrational transition frequency ωe\omega_eωe​, creating a series of lines that shift progressively to lower energies as JJJ increases.

  • The RRR-branch consists of transitions where the rotational quantum number increases by 1 (ΔJ=+1\Delta J = +1ΔJ=+1). These transitions appear at wavenumbers higher than ωe\omega_eωe​, resulting in a series of lines at progressively higher energies as JJJ increases.

In a spectrum, the PPP-branch lines appear on the lower wavenumber side of the fundamental vibrational frequency, while the RRR-branch lines appear on the higher wavenumber side. These branches provide a characteristic double-sided pattern centered around ωe\omega_eωe​, reflecting the rotational structure superimposed on the vibrational transition.

Problem 2¶

Measurement of pure rotational spectrum of H35^{35}35Cl molecule gave the following positions for the absorption lines:

ν~=(20.794cm−1)(J+1)−(0.000164cm−1)(J+1)3\tilde{\nu} = \left(20.794\textnormal{cm}^{-1}\right)\left(J+1\right) - \left(0.000164\textnormal{cm}^{-1}\right)\left(J+1\right)^3ν~=(20.794cm−1)(J+1)−(0.000164cm−1)(J+1)3
(39)

What is the equilibrium bond length and what is the value of the centrifugal distortion constant?

Solution

We first write the expression for B~\tilde{B}B~ and then use the definition of the moment of inertia III:

B~=h8π2cI=h8π2cμR02\tilde{B} = \frac{h}{8\pi^2cI} = \frac{h}{8\pi^2c\mu R_0^2}B~=8π2cIh​=8π2cμR02​h​
(40)

where μ\muμ is the reduced mass for the molecule and R0R_0R0​ is the equilibrium bond length. Solving for R0R_0R0​ gives:

R0=h8π2cμB~=129 pmR_0 = \sqrt{\frac{h}{8\pi^2c\mu\tilde{B}}} = 129\textnormal{ pm}R0​=8π2cμB~h​​=129 pm
(41)

The centrifugal distortion constant can be obtained by comparing the above equation with the equation for rovibronic coupling:

D~=4.1×10−5 cm−1\tilde{D} = 4.1\times 10^{-5}\textnormal{ cm}^{-1}D~=4.1×10−5 cm−1
(42)
